title: Extra-Wide Triple-Pedestal Farmhouse Table
summary: A nearly 8-foot farmhouse table on three boxed 2x4 pedestals with angled decorative cross supports, wide enough to need the third leg for real support.
description:
At close to 96" long, this table is wide enough that a standard two-pedestal base would sag in the middle under real load — the third, center pedestal is what makes a table this size structurally honest rather than just visually balanced. Build all three pedestals identically and check that a 2x6 slides cleanly into each pedestal's opening before you move on; a tight or loose fit on even one pedestal throws off the beam alignment down the line.

The angled cross braces on each pedestal are cut at 22-1/2° — an eighth-turn angle, not a standard 45° — so double-check your miter saw's angle setting against the cut list before running through all 24 of those pieces. The beams that run the length of the table get slid onto the assembled pedestals rather than boxed in around them, which is what lets this design go together and come apart again for moving a table this size through doorways.

tools.note: Double-check the 22-1/2° angle setting before cutting the 24 cross-support pieces.

materials.name: Pine project panels, 16" x 96"
materials.note: Or 1x lumber for the tabletop, at least 47-1/2" x 95-3/4" total.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 22-1/2° off square, long-point to long-point.
Both ends cut at 22-1/2° off square, long-point to long-point.
One end cut at 22-1/2° off square, longest-point measurement; other end square.
Both ends cut at 22-1/2° off square, long-point to long-point.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: This is a lot of repeat cuts, so set the saw once per angle and run every matching piece at that setting. From the 2x4s cut twelve outer leg pieces at 22-1/2" and twelve inner leg pieces at 8-1/2", all square; three outer top caps at 47", three middle top caps at 42-3/4" and three base caps at 44", each with both ends at 22-1/2° off square, long point to long point; and three tabletop frame cross members at 36-1/4". That is ten of the twelve supplied 8 ft 2x4s. From the 2x6s cut two long tabletop frame members at 94-1/4" first, since each needs a full 8 ft board, then two beams at 70" and twenty-four cross support pieces at 3-1/4" with one end cut 22-1/2° off square as a longest-point measurement and the other left square — that fills all five 2x6s. From the 1x2s cut six pedestal foot blocks at 3-1/2" and two short tabletop frame pieces at 47-1/4". Check your saw against a square at 22-1/2°, not the more familiar 45°, before you run the twenty-four cross supports.

steps.title: Build the six main leg pieces
steps.body: Each pedestal has two upright leg assemblies, so you are building six identical ones. Sandwich the 8-1/2" inner pieces between the 22-1/2" outer pieces so a slot is left down the middle, gluing and pinning with 2" brad nails and keeping every outside edge flush. Before the glue sets on the first one, slide an offcut of 2x6 into the slot and confirm it moves without forcing and without slop — the beams thread through these openings later, and a slot that is tight or loose on even one pedestal throws the whole base out of alignment. Build the remaining five against the first as a pattern rather than against the drawing.

steps.title: Add the first outer 2x4 layer
steps.body: Screw the first layer of outer 2x4 caps to each pedestal with 2-1/2" self-tapping screws: the 47" outer top cap across the top and the 44" base cap across the bottom, both cut with 22-1/2° ends long point to long point so their angled ends overhang the legs symmetrically. Predrill near the ends so the 2x4 does not split. Work on a flat surface and keep the two leg assemblies of each pedestal parallel and the same distance apart top and bottom, so the 2x6 slot still lines up through both. Repeat for all three pedestals and compare them against each other before moving on.

steps.title: Add the angled cross supports
steps.body: The twenty-four cross supports are 3-1/4" 2x6 pieces with one end cut at 22-1/2° and the other left square, and they stack into the angled fan on each pedestal face — eight per pedestal. Dry-lay a full set of eight on one pedestal before any glue and check that the fan closes evenly; if it does not, your saw is off the 22-1/2° and every remaining piece is wrong, which is exactly why the tool note warns about this angle. Attach with glue and 2" brad nails, working outward from the centre of each face so any accumulated error lands at the edges where it can be trimmed rather than in the middle where it shows.

steps.title: Attach the remaining bottom piece
steps.body: Add the remaining cap pieces to each pedestal with glue and 2" brad nails — the 42-3/4" pieces, one per pedestal, sitting inboard of the 47" caps already on. Note that the cut list calls these middle TOP caps while the original step called this the remaining BOTTOM piece; the plan contradicts itself, so set all three pedestals side by side and place them the same way whichever you choose, so the base reads as one piece. Finish by fixing the six 1x2 foot blocks, two per pedestal, under the base caps. Stand all three pedestals on a flat floor and confirm they are the same height before any of them meets a beam.

steps.title: Slide the legs onto the beams
steps.body: Lay the two 70" 2x6 beams parallel on the floor and mark all three pedestal positions on them, measuring outward from the centre of the beam so the spacing comes out symmetrical. The beams are shorter than the 94-1/4" tabletop frame, so the two end pedestals sit inboard of the table's ends rather than flush with them, and the centre pedestal is what keeps a top this wide from sagging between them. Slide each pedestal onto both beams through the slots you built in the first assembly step, working them along rather than forcing them. Once all three are positioned, fasten through the pedestal legs into the beams with 2" nails or 2-1/2" screws.

steps.title: Build the tabletop frame and attach the top
steps.body: Build the tabletop frame first: the two 94-1/4" 2x6 long members with the three 36-1/4" 2x4 cross members between them — one at each end and one at the centre — joined with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws. That gives a frame 47-1/4" wide, which is exactly the length of the two 1x2 short pieces that go across the ends. Measure both diagonals; at nearly 8 ft long a small error here opens a visible gap at the end of the top. Then lay your three 16" x 96" project panels across the frame with their edges tight, glue the panel joints, and fasten down with 2-1/2" screws driven up through the frame into the underside of the panels so nothing shows on the surface.

steps.title: Attach the base to the tabletop
steps.body: Turn the tabletop upside down on a padded floor and centre the three-pedestal base on it, measuring from the top's edges to the base at several points so the overhang is even all round — the top is 48" by 96" over a frame 47-1/4" by 94-1/4", so you are working to a small margin and an off-centre base shows immediately. Fasten up through the pedestal top caps into the tabletop frame with 2-1/2" self-tapping screws, at least two per pedestal, predrilling so nothing splits. Get help flipping it upright; a top this size is heavy, flexible along its length, and awkward to turn alone.

images.alt: Extra-wide farmhouse dining table on three boxed pedestal legs with angled cross supports
